tips: Eat a variety of fruits and vegetables — aim for at least 5 portions per day (WHO recommendation).
Choose whole grains over refined carbohydrates to support stable blood sugar levels.
Limit processed foods high in salt, sugar, and saturated fats.
Stay adequately hydrated — adults generally need 1.5–2 litres of water daily.
Include healthy protein sources such as legumes, fish, poultry, and nuts.
Limit alcohol consumption — no amount of alcohol is completely safe for health (WHO).
Reduce sodium intake to below 5g per day to help manage blood pressure (WHO).
Adults should aim for at least 150–300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week (WHO).
Include muscle-strengthening activities (e.g., resistance training) at least 2 days per week.
Reduce sedentary time — even breaking up prolonged sitting with short walks helps.
Children and adolescents should get at least 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity daily.
Regular walking, swimming, or cycling are safe options for most adults.
Physical activity helps manage weight, blood pressure, blood sugar, and mental health.
Adults should aim for 7–9 hours of sleep per night (CDC).
Maintain a consistent sleep schedule — go to bed and wake up at the same time daily.
Create a relaxing bedtime routine and a cool, dark, quiet sleep environment.
Limit screen exposure (phones, tablets) at least 1 hour before bed.
Avoid caffeine and heavy meals in the hours before bedtime.
Chronic sleep deprivation increases the risk of obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and mental health disorders.
Talk about your feelings — connecting with friends, family, or a counsellor is important.
Practice stress management techniques: deep breathing, mindfulness, meditation, or yoga.
Regular physical activity is one of the most effective ways to improve mood and reduce anxiety.
Take breaks and set healthy work-life boundaries.
Limit news and social media if it is causing anxiety.
Seek professional support early — mental health conditions are treatable.
Community connection and social support are powerful protective factors for mental wellbeing (WHO).
Wash hands frequently with soap and water for at least 20 seconds — especially before eating and after using the toilet (CDC).
Use alcohol-based hand sanitiser when soap and water are not available.
Cover your mouth and nose with a bent elbow or tissue when you cough or sneeze.
Avoid touching your face — eyes, nose, and mouth are entry points for germs.
Keep your home, kitchen, and bathroom clean and well-ventilated.
Safe food handling: store, prepare, and cook food safely to prevent foodborne illness.
Brush teeth twice daily and floss daily — oral health is closely linked to overall health.
Vaccines are one of the most effective public health tools available — they protect individuals and communities (WHO).
Keep up with recommended vaccine schedules for your age group and country.
Common vaccines for adults include: influenza (annual), COVID-19, tetanus booster, pneumococcal, shingles, and hepatitis B.
Travellers should check travel health requirements and recommended vaccines for their destination.
Vaccines for children (measles, polio, hepatitis B, DTP, etc.) are critical to prevent childhood illnesses.
Consult your GP or local health authority for a personalised vaccination schedule.
Regular blood pressure checks: high blood pressure often has no symptoms but is a leading risk factor for heart disease and stroke.
Blood glucose testing: type 2 diabetes often develops silently — early detection prevents complications.
Cholesterol checks: high cholesterol increases the risk of heart disease.
Cancer screenings: regular mammograms, cervical smear tests, colorectal screening, and skin checks are recommended based on age and risk.
Eye and dental check-ups should be performed regularly even if you feel well.
Speak with your healthcare provider to determine which screenings are appropriate for your age and risk factors.
Quit smoking — smoking is the single most preventable cause of cardiovascular disease (WHO).
Manage blood pressure: reduce salt, limit alcohol, exercise regularly, and take prescribed medications.
Maintain a healthy weight and waist circumference.
Manage blood cholesterol through diet (reduce saturated fats) and medication if needed.
Control blood sugar if you have diabetes.
Manage stress — chronic stress contributes to heart disease risk.
Regular aerobic exercise strengthens the heart and improves circulation.
Adequate calcium intake (1000–1200 mg/day for adults) supports bone density — found in dairy, leafy greens, and fortified foods.
Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption — safe sun exposure and supplements may be needed.
Weight-bearing exercise (walking, jogging, strength training) builds and maintains bone density.
Avoid smoking and excessive alcohol, which weaken bones.
Fall prevention is critical for older adults — remove trip hazards, improve lighting, and consider balance exercises.
Maintain a healthy body weight to reduce stress on joints.
Lose excess weight — a 5–7% reduction in body weight significantly reduces the risk of type 2 diabetes (CDC).
Get at least 150 minutes of moderate physical activity per week.
Choose high-fibre, low-glycaemic index foods (whole grains, legumes, vegetables).
Reduce consumption of sugary beverages, sweets, and processed snacks.
Get regular blood sugar checks, especially with a family history of diabetes.
Quit smoking — smoking increases the risk of type 2 diabetes.
